skill.name: jira
skill.description: Use Jira Cloud REST API v3 and JQL to search, create, update, comment on, assign, and transition Jira issues.
skill.summary: Manage Jira Cloud issues with REST API v3, JQL search, and ADF formatting guidance.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious exfiltration intent. Most static command and weak-crypto findings are false positives from markdown examples, but the helper script legitimately reads Jira credentials and sends authenticated write-capable requests to Jira Cloud.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Jira API Helper Can Modify Remote Issues
medium_findings.description: The helper script accepts GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE methods and sends authenticated requests to the configured Jira Cloud API. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but misuse can change or delete Jira data with the configured user permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly constructs authenticated urllib requests and exposes write-capable HTTP methods. The behavior is intentional for Jira automation, so the concern is permissioned misuse rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Credential Loading from Environment and .env
medium_findings.description: The script reads JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_API_TOKEN, and JIRA_DOMAIN from environment variables and loads missing values from a local .env file. This is normal for API tooling, but users must protect the token and avoid leaking command output or prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential reads and Basic Auth header construction are explicit in the script. No evidence shows credential exfiltration beyond requests to the configured Jira domain.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Command Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static findings point to fenced bash snippets and inline markdown examples in the skill documentation. They are user-facing examples, not executable code loaded by the skill itself, although users should still review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced markdown examples and curl documentation. There is no dynamic shell execution in those markdown files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings correspond to markdown text such as ADF document syntax, JQL examples, and Basic Auth base64 encoding examples. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic decision logic was found in the referenced documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script uses base64 only for HTTP Basic Auth encoding, not cryptographic protection. The markdown hits are syntax examples, not crypto implementations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper builds a Jira Cloud URL from configuration and sends requests with an Authorization header. This requires user trust because the configured token authorizes remote Jira operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL construction and Authorization header use are direct and easy to verify. The destination is the configured Jira domain, not an unknown third-party endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local .env File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper loads a .env file from the current working directory and copies missing key values into the process environment. This is standard API configuration behavior but handles secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read and environment assignment are explicit. The code only parses local key-value lines and does not transmit the file contents wholesale.

content.user_title: Manage Jira Issues from AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Jira work often requires exact API endpoints, JQL syntax, and rich text formatting. This skill provides focused Jira Cloud guidance and a helper script for authenticated issue operations.
actual_capabilities: Retrieve Jira issue details by issue key or endpoint.
Search Jira issues with documented JQL patterns and filters.
Create and update issues using Jira REST API v3 payload guidance.
Add comments and descriptions with Atlassian Document Format examples.
List transitions and move issues to another workflow status.
Assign or unassign issues with Jira account identifiers.
limitations: Requires a Jira Cloud domain, account email, and API token.
Does not include OAuth, Jira Server, or Data Center authentication flows.
Operations are limited by the permissions of the configured Jira user.
The helper script accepts arbitrary Jira API endpoints under the configured domain.

use_cases.title: Triage assigned work
use_cases.description: Find open tickets, inspect status, and identify recent updates for daily planning.

use_cases.title: Prepare release tracking
use_cases.description: Search issues by project, sprint, priority, label, or fix version before a release review.

use_cases.title: Automate ticket updates
use_cases.description: Create issues, add comments, update labels, or transition status from an AI-assisted workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Find my open issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Jira skill to build a JQL query for my unresolved assigned issues, sorted by priority and update date.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a ticket
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Jira skill to retrieve issue PROJ-123 with summary, status, assignee, labels, priority, and recent comments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a detailed task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Jira skill to draft a Jira task with a structured ADF description, acceptance criteria, priority, and labels.

prompt_templates.title: Transition and comment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Jira skill to list transitions for PROJ-123, choose the matching status, and add a concise ADF comment.

output_examples.input: Find my unresolved work for the current sprint.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a JQL query for assigned unresolved sprint issues, plus the recommended search command and fields to review.

output_examples.input: Create a bug ticket with reproduction steps.
output_examples.output: The skill prepares the issue fields, issue type, summary, labels, and rich text description in Jira-compatible ADF format.

output_examples.input: Move PROJ-123 to In Review and add a note.
output_examples.output: The skill first checks available transitions, then prepares the status update and comment request for Jira Cloud.

best_practices: Use a Jira API token with the minimum permissions needed for the workflow.
Review generated JQL and issue updates before running write operations.
Store credentials in environment variables or a protected local .env file.
anti_patterns: Do not use a personal admin token for routine ticket updates.
Do not run delete or transition requests without confirming the target issue key.
Do not paste Jira tokens into prompts, tickets, comments, or shared logs.

faq.question: Does this skill work with Jira Cloud?
faq.answer: Yes. The references and helper script target Jira Cloud REST API v3.

faq.question: Does it support Jira Server or Data Center?
faq.answer: No. The documented endpoints and authentication are for Jira Cloud.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It needs JIRA_DOMAIN, JIRA_EMAIL, and JIRA_API_TOKEN environment values.

faq.question: Can it create or update Jira tickets?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents create, update, comment, assign, and transition workflows.

faq.question: Can it search Jira issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes JQL operators, fields, functions, and common query patterns.

faq.question: Does it store Jira data externally?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The helper sends requests only to the configured Jira Cloud domain.
